title: Combination sum II
description: Find unique combinations that sum to target

examples.explanation: Step 1: Sort candidates -> [1,1,2,5,6,7,10]. Step 2: Initialize results = [], current = [], index = 0, sum = 0. Step 3: Recursive calls: Include 1: current=[1], sum=1, index=1. Include 1 again: current=[1,1], sum=2, index=2. Include 2: current=[1,1,2], sum=4, index=3. Include 5: current=[1,1,2,5], sum=9 >8, backtrack. Exclude 5, include 6: current=[1,1,6], sum=8, add [1,1,6] to results. Backtrack, exclude 6, include 7: sum=9>8, backtrack. Exclude 7, include 10: sum=11>8, backtrack. Backtrack to [1], exclude second 1, include 2: current=[1,2], sum=3, index=3. Include 5: current=[1,2,5], sum=8, add [1,2,5]. Backtrack, exclude 5, include 6: sum=9>8, backtrack. Include 7: sum=10>8, backtrack. Include 10: sum=13>8, backtrack. Backtrack to [], exclude first 1, include second 1: current=[1], sum=1, index=2 (skip first 1). Include 2: current=[1,2], sum=3, index=3. Include 5: sum=8, add [1,2,5] (duplicate, skip). Include 6: sum=9>8, backtrack. Include 7: sum=8, add [1,7]. Include 10: sum=11>8, backtrack. Backtrack to [], exclude both 1s, include 2: current=[2], sum=2, index=3. Include 5: sum=7, index=4. Include 6: sum=8, add [2,6]. Continue exploring other branches, no more valid combinations. Final results after duplicate removal: [[1,1,6],[1,2,5],[1,7],[2,6]].

Step 1: Sort candidates -> [1,2,2,2,5]. Step 2: Initialize results = [], current = [], index = 0, sum = 0. Step 3: Recursive calls: Include 1: current=[1], sum=1, index=1. Include 2: current=[1,2], sum=3, index=2. Include 2: current=[1,2,2], sum=5, add [1,2,2]. Backtrack, exclude second 2, include third 2: same sum=5, add [1,2,2] (duplicate, skip). Include 5: sum=6>5, backtrack. Backtrack to [1], exclude 2, include next 2: same as before, skip. Include 5: sum=6>5, backtrack. Backtrack to [], exclude 1, include 2: current=[2], sum=2, index=2. Include 2: current=[2,2], sum=4, index=3. Include 2: current=[2,2,2], sum=6>5, backtrack. Include 5: sum=7>5, backtrack. Backtrack to [2], exclude second 2, include third 2: same as before, skip. Include 5: sum=7>5, backtrack. Backtrack to [], exclude first 2, include second 2: same as [2] branch, skip. Include third 2: same, skip. Include 5: current=[5], sum=5, add [5]. Final results: [[1,2,2],[5]].

approaches.name: Brute Force Backtracking with Duplicate Checking
approaches.summary: Generate all possible combinations by recursively exploring each candidate, including duplicates, and then filter out duplicate combinations using a set or sorting. This is straightforward but inefficient due to redundant work.
approaches.timeComplexity: O(2^n * n) due to generating all subsets and checking duplicates
approaches.spaceComplexity: O(n) for recursion depth and storage of combinations

ladders.title: Full Problem — Combination Sum II with Brute Force Backtracking and Duplicate Checking
ladders.desc: Find all unique combinations where candidate numbers sum to target, each candidate used at most once, using exhaustive backtracking with duplicate filtering.
ladders.explanation: This approach solves the Combination Sum II problem by generating all possible combinations through recursive backtracking, then filtering out duplicates. The intuition is to explore every possible subset of candidates (considering each candidate can be used at most once) and check if their sum equals the target. Since candidates may contain duplicates, we need to ensure the final combinations are unique. We achieve this by sorting the candidates initially and using a set to store combinations (or checking for duplicates during backtracking). The algorithm works by recursively including or excluding each candidate, building combinations, and when the sum equals target, adding the combination to results after checking for duplicates. Time complexity is O(2^n * n) because we generate all subsets (2^n possibilities) and for each valid combination, we may spend O(n) time to check/store it. Space complexity is O(n) for recursion depth and storing combinations. This connects to L1 as it uses the backtracking subroutine to explore combinations. Common mistakes include not handling duplicates properly (leading to duplicate combinations in results), not sorting candidates initially, and inefficient duplicate checking. Real code pattern: sort candidates, define recursive backtrack function that explores include/exclude decisions, track current combination and sum, add to results when sum==target and combination not already in set.

examples.trace: Step 1: Sort candidates -> [1,1,2,5,6,7,10]. Step 2: Initialize results = [], current = [], index = 0, sum = 0. Step 3: Recursive calls: Include 1: current=[1], sum=1, index=1. Include 1 again: current=[1,1], sum=2, index=2. Include 2: current=[1,1,2], sum=4, index=3. Include 5: current=[1,1,2,5], sum=9 >8, backtrack. Exclude 5, include 6: current=[1,1,6], sum=8, add [1,1,6] to results. Backtrack, exclude 6, include 7: sum=9>8, backtrack. Exclude 7, include 10: sum=11>8, backtrack. Backtrack to [1], exclude second 1, include 2: current=[1,2], sum=3, index=3. Include 5: current=[1,2,5], sum=8, add [1,2,5]. Backtrack, exclude 5, include 6: sum=9>8, backtrack. Include 7: sum=10>8, backtrack. Include 10: sum=13>8, backtrack. Backtrack to [], exclude first 1, include second 1: current=[1], sum=1, index=2 (skip first 1). Include 2: current=[1,2], sum=3, index=3. Include 5: sum=8, add [1,2,5] (duplicate, skip). Include 6: sum=9>8, backtrack. Include 7: sum=8, add [1,7]. Include 10: sum=11>8, backtrack. Backtrack to [], exclude both 1s, include 2: current=[2], sum=2, index=3. Include 5: sum=7, index=4. Include 6: sum=8, add [2,6]. Continue exploring other branches, no more valid combinations. Final results after duplicate removal: [[1,1,6],[1,2,5],[1,7],[2,6]].

Step 1: Sort candidates -> [1,2,2,2,5]. Step 2: Initialize results = [], current = [], index = 0, sum = 0. Step 3: Recursive calls: Include 1: current=[1], sum=1, index=1. Include 2: current=[1,2], sum=3, index=2. Include 2: current=[1,2,2], sum=5, add [1,2,2]. Backtrack, exclude second 2, include third 2: same sum=5, add [1,2,2] (duplicate, skip). Include 5: sum=6>5, backtrack. Backtrack to [1], exclude 2, include next 2: same as before, skip. Include 5: sum=6>5, backtrack. Backtrack to [], exclude 1, include 2: current=[2], sum=2, index=2. Include 2: current=[2,2], sum=4, index=3. Include 2: current=[2,2,2], sum=6>5, backtrack. Include 5: sum=7>5, backtrack. Backtrack to [2], exclude second 2, include third 2: same as before, skip. Include 5: sum=7>5, backtrack. Backtrack to [], exclude first 2, include second 2: same as [2] branch, skip. Include third 2: same, skip. Include 5: current=[5], sum=5, add [5]. Final results: [[1,2,2],[5]].

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Backtracking to Generate All Combinations
ladders.desc: Recursively generate all possible combinations from a list of candidates, each used at most once.
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine is the core of the brute force approach, responsible for exploring all possible combinations through recursion. The intuition is to treat the problem as a decision tree: at each step, we decide whether to include the current candidate in the combination or skip it. By recursively exploring both choices, we cover all subsets of the candidates. The algorithm starts from an index in the candidate list, includes the candidate, updates the sum, and recurses to the next index; then backtracks by excluding the candidate and recurses. This ensures each candidate is considered at most once per path. Time complexity is O(2^n) as we generate all subsets. Space complexity is O(n) for recursion depth. This feeds into L0 by providing the mechanism to build combinations, which are then checked for sum and duplicates. Common mistakes include not incrementing index properly when including a candidate (leading to reuse), and not backtracking correctly (failing to remove candidate from current combination). Real code pattern: define backtrack(start, current, current_sum) that loops from start to end, includes candidates[i], recurses with i+1, then removes candidate and continues loop.

examples.trace: Step 1: Start with index=0, current=[], sum=0. Step 2: Include 1: current=[1], index=1. Include 2: current=[1,2], index=2. Include 3: current=[1,2,3], index=3, add [1,2,3]. Backtrack: remove 3, current=[1,2], index=2 done, backtrack remove 2, current=[1], index=1. Include 3: current=[1,3], index=3, add [1,3]. Backtrack remove 3, current=[1], index=1 done, backtrack remove 1, current=[], index=0. Include 2: current=[2], index=2. Include 3: current=[2,3], index=3, add [2,3]. Backtrack remove 3, current=[2], index=2 done, backtrack remove 2, current=[], index=0. Include 3: current=[3], index=3, add [3]. Backtrack remove 3, current=[], index=0 done. Add empty combination [] at start or end. Final: all subsets.

Step 1: Index=0, current=[]. Include first 1: current=[1], index=1. Include second 1: current=[1,1], index=2, add [1,1]. Backtrack remove second 1, current=[1], index=1 done, backtrack remove first 1, current=[], index=0. Include second 1: current=[1], index=2, add [1] (but duplicate with first, will be handled in L0). Backtrack remove second 1, current=[], index=0 done. Add [].

ladders.title: Core Logic — Checking and Filtering Duplicate Combinations
ladders.desc: Identify and remove duplicate combinations from a list of combinations.
ladders.explanation: This is the central technique to ensure uniqueness in the results, as candidates may have duplicates. The intuition is that after generating all combinations, some may be identical due to duplicate candidates in the input. We filter these out by using a data structure that does not allow duplicates, such as a set, or by sorting each combination and using a set of tuples. The algorithm involves: after generating a combination that sums to target, convert it to a tuple (since lists are not hashable), add it to a set to check for duplicates, and only add to results if not already present. Alternatively, sort the candidates initially and skip duplicates during backtracking (more efficient, but for brute force, post-filtering is simpler). Time complexity is O(k * n) where k is number of combinations and n is average length, for checking duplicates. Space complexity is O(k * n) for storing combinations. This connects to L1 by taking the generated combinations and ensuring uniqueness before output. Common mistakes include not converting combinations to tuples before adding to set (leading to unhashable type error), or not sorting combinations consistently before comparison. Real code pattern: maintain a set 'seen', when a valid combination is found, sort it (if not already sorted), convert to tuple, if not in seen, add to results and seen.

examples.trace: Step 1: Initialize seen = set(), results = []. Step 2: Process [1,2]: sort -> [1,2], tuple -> (1,2), not in seen, add to results and seen. Step 3: Process [2,1]: sort -> [1,2], tuple -> (1,2), already in seen, skip. Step 4: Process [1,2]: sort -> [1,2], tuple -> (1,2), already in seen, skip. Step 5: Process [3]: sort -> [3], tuple -> (3), not in seen, add. Final results: [[1,2],[3]].

ladders.title: Building Block — Recursive Summation of a List
ladders.desc: Compute the sum of elements in a list using recursion.
ladders.explanation: This intermediate concept is needed to check if a combination sums to the target during backtracking. The intuition is to break down the sum calculation recursively: the sum of a list is the first element plus the sum of the rest of the list. This mirrors the recursive nature of backtracking. The algorithm defines a function sum_list(lst) that returns 0 if lst is empty, otherwise returns lst[0] + sum_list(lst[1:]). Time complexity is O(n) where n is list length, as each element is visited once. Space complexity is O(n) for recursion depth. This connects to L2 by providing a way to compute the sum of a generated combination, which is then compared to the target. Common mistakes include not handling empty list case (leading to index error), and inefficient recursion for large lists (but for this problem, lists are small). Real code pattern: def sum_list(lst): if not lst: return 0 else: return lst[0] + sum_list(lst[1:]).

examples.trace: Step 1: sum_list([1,2,3]) -> 1 + sum_list([2,3]). Step 2: sum_list([2,3]) -> 2 + sum_list([3]). Step 3: sum_list([3]) -> 3 + sum_list([]). Step 4: sum_list([]) -> 0. Step 5: Back substitute: 3 + 0 = 3, 2 + 3 = 5, 1 + 5 = 6.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Sorting a List of Numbers
ladders.desc: Arrange a list of integers in non-decreasing order.
ladders.explanation: This fundamental operation is used to prepare candidates for duplicate checking and to ensure consistent combination representation. The intuition is that sorting brings order, making it easier to compare combinations and skip duplicates. The algorithm can use built-in sort functions (e.g., sorted() in Python) which typically run in O(n log n) time. For this problem, we sort the candidate list initially so that during backtracking or post-filtering, combinations can be compared consistently. Time complexity is O(n log n) for sorting n candidates. Space complexity is O(n) for the sorted list. This connects to L3 by providing sorted input for sum calculation and L2 for duplicate filtering. Common mistakes include sorting in-place when original order needs preservation, or not sorting before duplicate checks. Real code pattern: sorted_candidates = sorted(candidates).

examples.trace: Step 1: Input list: [3,1,2]. Step 2: Apply sorting algorithm (e.g., quicksort or built-in): compare elements and rearrange. Step 3: Sorted order: 1,2,3. Output: [1,2,3].
Step 1: Input: [5,5,1,5]. Step 2: Sorting process: identify 1 as smallest, move to front. Step 3: Remaining [5,5,5] are already in order. Output: [1,5,5,5].

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Understanding Lists and Basic Recursion
ladders.desc: Learn how lists work and the idea of recursive function calls.
ladders.explanation: This is the simplest prerequisite, essential for beginners to grasp before tackling the problem. Lists are ordered collections of items; here, we use lists of integers. Recursion is a technique where a function calls itself to solve smaller instances of a problem. The intuition is to break a problem into similar subproblems. For example, to compute the sum of a list recursively: if the list is empty, sum is 0; otherwise, sum is first element plus sum of rest of list. This foundation connects to L4 as lists are sorted and L3 as recursion is used for summation. Common mistakes include infinite recursion (no base case) and not understanding list indexing. Real code pattern: def recursive_sum(lst): if len(lst) == 0: return 0 else: return lst[0] + recursive_sum(lst[1:]).

examples.input: Explain list [1,2,3] and recursive sum step-by-step
examples.trace: Step 1: List representation: positions 0:1, 1:2, 2:3. Step 2: Base case: empty list sum is 0. Step 3: Recursive case: sum(lst) = first element + sum(rest). Step 4: For [1,2,3]: first=1, rest=[2,3]. Step 5: Compute sum([2,3]): first=2, rest=[3]. Step 6: Compute sum([3]): first=3, rest=[]. Step 7: sum([])=0. Step 8: Back substitute: 3+0=3, 2+3=5, 1+5=6.

examples.input: Show recursive call stack for sum([4,5])
examples.trace: Step 1: Call sum([4,5]). Step 2: Check base case: list not empty. Step 3: Return 4 + sum([5]). Step 4: Call sum([5]): not empty, return 5 + sum([]). Step 5: Call sum([]): empty, return 0. Step 6: Unwind: 5 + 0 = 5, then 4 + 5 = 9.

testCases.input: List [1,2], what is element at index 0?
What is the sum of [] using recursion?
How many recursive calls for sum([1,2,3,4])?

testCases.expected: A function that calls itself to solve smaller problems.

approaches.name: Backtracking with Sorting and Skip Duplicates
approaches.summary: Sort the candidates first, then use backtracking to build combinations while skipping duplicate candidates at the same recursion level to avoid generating duplicate combinations. This optimizes by pruning early.
approaches.timeComplexity: O(2^n) in worst case, but typically faster due to pruning
approaches.spaceComplexity: O(n) for recursion depth and temporary combination storage

ladders.title: Full Problem — Combination Sum II with Backtracking, Sorting, and Duplicate Skipping
ladders.desc: Find all unique combinations in candidates that sum to target, where each candidate can be used at most once per combination.
ladders.explanation: This problem requires finding all unique combinations from a list of candidates that sum to a target value, with the constraint that each candidate can be used at most once per combination. The key challenge is avoiding duplicate combinations when the input contains duplicate numbers. The solution uses backtracking with sorting and duplicate skipping. First, sort the candidates to group duplicates together. Then, use a recursive backtracking function that explores two possibilities at each step: include the current candidate or skip it. When including, add the candidate to the current combination, reduce the target by its value, and recurse with the next index. When skipping, move to the next candidate. To avoid duplicate combinations, when skipping a candidate, skip all subsequent duplicates at the same recursion level. This ensures that each unique combination is generated exactly once. The base cases are when the target becomes zero (add the current combination to results) or when the target becomes negative or we run out of candidates (prune). Time complexity is O(2^n) in the worst case due to exploring all subsets, but pruning reduces this in practice. Space complexity is O(n) for recursion depth and the temporary combination. This approach efficiently handles duplicates by leveraging sorting and careful skipping, making it optimal for this problem.

examples.trace:
Step 1: Sort candidates: [1,1,2,5,6,7,10].
Step 2: Start backtracking with index=0, target=8, combination=[].
Step 3: At index=0 (value=1): Include: combination=[1], target=7, recurse with index=1.
  Step 3.1: At index=1 (value=1): Include: combination=[1,1], target=6, recurse with index=2.
    Step 3.1.1: At index=2 (value=2): Include: combination=[1,1,2], target=4, recurse with index=3.
      Step 3.1.1.1: At index=3 (value=5): Skip (target 4 < 5).
      Step 3.1.1.2: Skip duplicates: move to index=4.
    Step 3.1.2: At index=2 (value=2): Skip, move to index=3.
  Step 3.2: At index=1 (value=1): Skip, but skip all duplicates: move to index=2 (since next is 2, different).
Step 4: At index=0 (value=1): Skip, move to index=1 (duplicate of 1), skip all duplicates: move to index=2.
Step 5: At index=2 (value=2): Include: combination=[2], target=6, recurse with index=3.
  Step 5.1: At index=3 (value=5): Include: combination=[2,5], target=1, recurse with index=4.
    Step 5.1.1: At index=4 (value=6): Skip (target 1 < 6).
    Step 5.1.2: Skip duplicates: move to index=5.
  Step 5.2: At index=3 (value=5): Skip, move to index=4.
Step 6: Continue exploring other branches, resulting in combinations: [1,1,6] (from index 0,1,4), [1,2,5] (from index 0,2,3), [1,7] (from index 0,5), [2,6] (from index 2,4).

Step 1: Sort candidates: [1,2,2,2,5].
Step 2: Start backtracking with index=0, target=5, combination=[].
Step 3: At index=0 (value=1): Include: combination=[1], target=4, recurse with index=1.
  Step 3.1: At index=1 (value=2): Include: combination=[1,2], target=2, recurse with index=2.
    Step 3.1.1: At index=2 (value=2): Include: combination=[1,2,2], target=0, add to results: [[1,2,2]].
    Step 3.1.2: At index=2 (value=2): Skip, and skip all duplicates: move to index=5 (end).
  Step 3.2: At index=1 (value=2): Skip, and skip all duplicates: move to index=4 (value=5).
Step 4: At index=0 (value=1): Skip, move to index=1 (duplicate? no, different value 2), but skip all duplicates of 1: none, so move to index=1.
Step 5: At index=1 (value=2): Include: combination=[2], target=3, recurse with index=2.
  Step 5.1: At index=2 (value=2): Include: combination=[2,2], target=1, recurse with index=3.
    Step 5.1.1: At index=3 (value=2): Skip (target 1 < 2).
    Step 5.1.2: Skip duplicates: move to index=4.
  Step 5.2: At index=2 (value=2): Skip, and skip all duplicates: move to index=4.
Step 6: At index=1 (value=2): Skip, and skip all duplicates: move to index=4.
Step 7: At index=4 (value=5): Include: combination=[5], target=0, add to results: [[1,2,2],[5]].
Step 8: Skip index=4, end. Result: [[1,2,2],[5]].

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Backtracking Function with Duplicate Skipping
ladders.desc: Implement the recursive backtracking function that builds combinations and skips duplicates at the same level.
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine is the core recursive function that explores all possible combinations by including or skipping candidates, while ensuring duplicates are skipped to avoid redundant combinations. The function takes parameters: the current index in the sorted candidates list, the remaining target sum, the current combination being built, and the results list. At each call, it checks base cases: if the target is zero, add the current combination to results; if the target is negative or index is out of bounds, return to prune. Otherwise, it explores two paths: include the current candidate and recurse with the next index and reduced target, or skip the current candidate. When skipping, it must skip all subsequent duplicate candidates at the same recursion level to prevent generating duplicate combinations. This is done by incrementing the index until a different candidate is found. The intuition is that at a given level, if we skip a candidate, we should also skip all its duplicates to avoid exploring symmetric branches that would yield the same combinations. Time complexity for this function is O(2^n) in worst case as it explores all subsets, but duplicate skipping reduces branches. Space complexity is O(n) for recursion depth and combination storage. This sub-routine feeds into the full problem by being called from the main function after sorting candidates.

examples.input: candidates = [1,1,2], target = 3, start index=0, current combination=[], results=[]
examples.trace:
Step 1: At index=0 (value=1), target=3, combination=[].
Step 2: Include path: combination=[1], target=2, recurse with index=1.
  Step 2.1: At index=1 (value=1), target=2, combination=[1].
    Step 2.1.1: Include: combination=[1,1], target=1, recurse with index=2.
      Step 2.1.1.1: At index=2 (value=2), target=1, combination=[1,1]. Skip (target 1 < 2).
      Step 2.1.1.2: Skip duplicates: index=3 (end).
    Step 2.1.2: Skip index=1, and skip duplicates: move to index=2 (value=2, different).
  Step 2.2: At index=1 (value=1), target=2, combination=[1]. Skip, and skip duplicates: move to index=2.
Step 3: At index=0 (value=1), target=3, combination=[]. Skip, and skip duplicates: move to index=2 (since index=1 is duplicate).
Step 4: At index=2 (value=2), target=3, combination=[]. Include: combination=[2], target=1, recurse with index=3.
  Step 4.1: Index=3, target=1: return (out of bounds).
Step 5: Skip index=2, end. Results after all calls: [[1,2]] from include path at index=0 and index=2.

examples.input: candidates = [2,2,2], target = 4, start index=0, current combination=[], results=[]
examples.trace:
Step 1: At index=0 (value=2), target=4, combination=[].
Step 2: Include: combination=[2], target=2, recurse with index=1.
  Step 2.1: At index=1 (value=2), target=2, combination=[2].
    Step 2.1.1: Include: combination=[2,2], target=0, add to results: [[2,2]].
    Step 2.1.2: Skip index=1, and skip duplicates: move to index=3 (end).
  Step 2.2: Skip index=1, and skip duplicates: move to index=3.
Step 3: At index=0 (value=2), target=4, combination=[]. Skip, and skip duplicates: move to index=3.
Step 4: End. Results: [[2,2]].

ladders.title: Core Logic — Skipping Duplicates in Sorted Array During Backtracking
ladders.desc: Implement the logic to skip duplicate candidates at the same recursion level to avoid generating duplicate combinations.
ladders.explanation: This core logic focuses on the mechanism to skip duplicate candidates during the backtracking process to ensure unique combinations. After sorting the candidates, duplicates are adjacent. During recursion, when we decide to skip a candidate at a given level, we must also skip all subsequent candidates with the same value at that level. This prevents exploring multiple branches that would lead to identical combinations. For example, with candidates [1,1,2], if we skip the first '1', we should also skip the second '1' at the same level to avoid symmetric paths. The implementation involves, in the skip branch of the recursion, incrementing the index while the next candidate equals the current candidate. This is done after the recursive call for inclusion and before the recursive call for skipping. The intuition is that each unique combination corresponds to a specific multiset of candidates, and by skipping duplicates at the same level, we ensure each multiset is considered only once. Time complexity for this step is O(n) in worst case for skipping, but it reduces the overall exponential search space. Space complexity is O(1) for the skipping operation. This logic is critical in the key sub-routine to handle duplicates efficiently.

examples.trace:
Step 1: At index=0, value=1.
Step 2: Decide to skip this candidate.
Step 3: Check next index=1: value=1, which equals current value 1.
Step 4: Increment index to 1, then check index=2: value=2, which is different from 1.
Step 5: Stop skipping at index=2. New index for skip branch is 2.

Step 1: At index=1, value=3.
Step 2: Decide to skip this candidate.
Step 3: Check next index=2: value=3, equals current value 3.
Step 4: Increment index to 2, then check index=3: value=4, different from 3.
Step 5: Stop skipping at index=3. New index for skip branch is 3.

ladders.title: Building Block — Recursive Backtracking for Subset Sum with Pruning
ladders.desc: Implement a recursive backtracking function to find all subsets that sum to a target, without handling duplicates.
ladders.explanation: This building block involves creating a recursive backtracking function that explores all subsets of a list of candidates to find those that sum to a target value. The function does not handle duplicates; it assumes all candidates are unique. It uses depth-first search: at each step, consider including the current candidate in the combination or skipping it. If included, add the candidate to the current combination, subtract its value from the target, and recurse with the next index. If skipped, recurse with the next index without modifying the combination. Base cases: if the target becomes zero, add the current combination to results; if the target becomes negative or the index is out of bounds, return to prune invalid paths. The intuition is to systematically explore all possible combinations (subsets) by making binary decisions at each candidate, similar to generating the power set. Time complexity is O(2^n) as it explores all subsets. Space complexity is O(n) for recursion depth and combination storage. This block is essential for the core logic, as adding duplicate skipping extends this to handle duplicates. Common mistakes include not pruning when target is negative (leading to extra recursion) or not copying the combination when adding to results (causing reference issues).

examples.input: candidates = [1,2,3], target = 3, start index=0, combination=[], results=[]
examples.trace:
Step 1: At index=0 (value=1), target=3, combination=[].
Step 2: Include: combination=[1], target=2, recurse with index=1.
  Step 2.1: At index=1 (value=2), target=2, combination=[1].
    Step 2.1.1: Include: combination=[1,2], target=0, add to results: [[1,2]].
    Step 2.1.2: Skip: recurse with index=2, combination=[1], target=2.
      Step 2.1.2.1: At index=2 (value=3), target=2, combination=[1]. Skip (target 2 < 3).
  Step 2.2: Skip: recurse with index=2, combination=[], target=3.
Step 3: At index=0 (value=1), target=3, combination=[]. Skip: recurse with index=1, combination=[], target=3.
  Step 3.1: At index=1 (value=2), target=3, combination=[].
    Step 3.1.1: Include: combination=[2], target=1, recurse with index=2.
      Step 3.1.1.1: At index=2 (value=3), target=1, combination=[2]. Skip (target 1 < 3).
    Step 3.1.2: Skip: recurse with index=2, combination=[], target=3.
      Step 3.1.2.1: At index=2 (value=3), target=3, combination=[]. Include: combination=[3], target=0, add to results: [[1,2],[3]].
Step 4: End. Results: [[1,2],[3]].

examples.input: candidates = [2,4], target = 6, start index=0, combination=[], results=[]
examples.trace:
Step 1: At index=0 (value=2), target=6, combination=[].
Step 2: Include: combination=[2], target=4, recurse with index=1.
  Step 2.1: At index=1 (value=4), target=4, combination=[2].
    Step 2.1.1: Include: combination=[2,4], target=0, add to results: [[2,4]].
    Step 2.1.2: Skip: recurse with index=2, combination=[2], target=4. Index out of bounds, return.
  Step 2.2: Skip: recurse with index=2, combination=[], target=6. Index out of bounds, return.
Step 3: At index=0 (value=2), target=6, combination=[]. Skip: recurse with index=1, combination=[], target=6.
  Step 3.1: At index=1 (value=4), target=6, combination=[].
    Step 3.1.1: Include: combination=[4], target=2, recurse with index=2. Index out of bounds, return.
    Step 3.1.2: Skip: recurse with index=2, combination=[], target=6. Index out of bounds, return.
Step 4: End. Results: [[2,4]].

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Sorting an Array and Iterating with Index Control
ladders.desc: Sort an array of numbers and implement iteration with index manipulation for skipping elements.
ladders.explanation: This basic operation involves sorting an array of numbers in non-decreasing order and then performing iteration with controlled index increments, particularly for skipping duplicate elements. Sorting is done using a standard algorithm like quicksort or mergesort, resulting in O(n log n) time. Once sorted, duplicates become adjacent, allowing efficient skipping during iteration. The iteration uses a while loop or for loop with an index variable that can be incremented conditionally. For example, to skip duplicates, after processing an element, increment the index until the next element has a different value. This operation is fundamental for the building block, as it prepares the data for backtracking by grouping duplicates. The intuition is that sorting brings order, making it easier to identify and handle duplicates without extra data structures. Time complexity for sorting is O(n log n), and for iteration, O(n). Space complexity is O(1) for in-place sorting or O(n) for out-of-place. Common mistakes include not sorting before skipping or incorrectly updating the index during iteration. This operation connects to higher levels by enabling the duplicate skipping logic in backtracking.

examples.input: Array = [3,1,2,1,3], sort and iterate to skip duplicates starting from index 0
examples.output: Sorted: [1,1,2,3,3]; after skipping duplicates of 1, index moves to 2
examples.trace:
Step 1: Sort the array: [1,1,2,3,3].
Step 2: Start at index=0, value=1.
Step 3: Process element at index=0.
Step 4: To skip duplicates of 1, increment index while next element equals 1.
  - Index=1: value=1, equals 1, so increment to index=2.
  - Index=2: value=2, different from 1, stop.
Step 5: New index is 2.

examples.input: Array = [5,5,5], sort and iterate to skip duplicates starting from index 0
examples.output: Sorted: [5,5,5]; after skipping duplicates of 5, index moves to 3
examples.trace:
Step 1: Sort the array: [5,5,5] (already sorted).
Step 2: Start at index=0, value=5.
Step 3: Process element at index=0.
Step 4: To skip duplicates of 5, increment index while next element equals 5.
  - Index=1: value=5, equals 5, so increment to index=2.
  - Index=2: value=5, equals 5, so increment to index=3.
  - Index=3: out of bounds, stop.
Step 5: New index is 3.

testCases.expected: Sorted: [7,7,8,8]; new index after skipping 7s from index 1: 2
Sorted: [2,2,3,3]; new index after skipping 3s from index 2: 4

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Understanding Arrays, Sorting, and Basic Iteration
ladders.desc: Learn to work with arrays, sort them, and iterate through elements using indices.
ladders.explanation: This foundation covers basic operations on arrays: creating, accessing, and modifying arrays of numbers. An array is a data structure that stores elements in contiguous memory locations, accessible by indices starting from 0. Sorting is the process of arranging elements in a specific order, such as ascending order for numbers. Common sorting algorithms include bubble sort (O(n^2)) for simplicity or built-in sorts (O(n log n)) for efficiency. Iteration involves traversing the array using a loop, such as a for loop or while loop, to access each element sequentially. For example, to print all elements, use a loop that runs from index 0 to length-1. This concept is crucial for the basic operation, as it underpins sorting and index manipulation. The intuition is that arrays provide a way to organize data, sorting brings structure, and iteration allows processing. Time complexity for iteration is O(n), and for sorting, it depends on the algorithm. Space complexity is O(1) for in-place operations. Common mistakes include off-by-one errors in loops or not handling empty arrays. This foundation is essential for beginners to progress to more complex algorithms.

examples.input: Array = [5,2,8,1], sort in ascending order and iterate to print elements
examples.trace:
Step 1: Start with array [5,2,8,1].
Step 2: Sort the array in ascending order: compare and swap elements until ordered. Using a simple sort: 
  - Compare 5 and 2: swap to [2,5,8,1].
  - Compare 5 and 8: no swap.
  - Compare 8 and 1: swap to [2,5,1,8].
  - Continue until sorted: final [1,2,5,8].
Step 3: Iterate through the sorted array using a for loop with index i from 0 to 3.
  - i=0: access element 1, print 1.
  - i=1: access element 2, print 2.
  - i=2: access element 5, print 5.
  - i=3: access element 8, print 8.
Step 4: Output: printed sequence 1,2,5,8.

examples.input: Array = [3,3,1], sort and iterate to count duplicates of the first element
examples.trace:
Step 1: Start with array [3,3,1].
Step 2: Sort in ascending order: [1,3,3].
Step 3: Iterate to count duplicates of the first element (value=1).
  - Initialize count=0.
  - Start from index=1 (since index=0 is the first element).
  - i=1: value=3, not equal to 1, so stop.
Step 4: Count remains 0. No duplicates of 1.

testCases.input: array = [3,3,1], sort and count duplicates of first element
array = [4,4,4], sort and find index of last element
array = [7,1,9], sort and access element at index 1
array = [0,5,0], sort and iterate to sum elements
array = [2,1,3], sort and check if sorted
array = [1,2,3,4], sort and find middle element
